title: Measuring Factuality: Self-Consistency
concept: Self-consistency evaluates factuality by generating multiple paths; if the model outputs different answers every run, its confidence is low.

exercises.prompt: What does checking an AI's 'self-consistency' help us measure?
exercises.explain: Self-consistency tests if an AI repeatedly arrives at the same conclusion, which is a key way to measure how factual its output is.

cards.title: Can We Trust the AI?
cards.body: Have you ever asked an AI a question, got an answer, and then asked it again only to get a completely different story? When AIs make things up, they are often inconsistent. We can actually use this weakness to measure how trustworthy their answers are!

cards.title: What is Self-Consistency?
cards.body: Self-consistency is a technique where we prompt an AI multiple times to solve the same problem. If it generates the same answer through different reasoning paths, the answer is highly likely to be factual. If it outputs wild, contradictory answers each time, it is likely hallucinating.

cards.title: The Directions Test
cards.body: Imagine asking five different friends for directions to the park. If they all point to the exact same street, you can be very confident they are correct. But if they point in five completely different directions, it is a clear sign that nobody actually knows the way. Self-consistency works the same way: we ask the AI the same question multiple times to see if its answers agree.

cards.title: Consistency in Action
cards.body:
We ask an AI: "What is the capital of Canada?" 
Run 1: "Ottawa"
Run 2: "Ottawa"
Run 3: "Ottawa"
Since all runs agree, the self-consistency score is 100%. We can trust this output! If it said "Toronto" in Run 2 and "Vancouver" in Run 3, we would know the AI is guessing.

cards.title: The Speed Trap
cards.body: You might think that if an AI answers a question quickly, it must be correct. In reality, AIs will confidently state incorrect facts with high speed. True factuality is measured by agreement across multiple independent attempts, not by how fast or confident a single answer looks.

cards.title: Consistent vs. Inconsistent Responses
cards.body: Comparing how we evaluate AI trust based on the alignment of its answers.

left.text: Three runs all conclude that the answer is 'Paris'. High reliability.

right.text: Three runs conclude 'Paris', 'London', and 'Rome'. Low reliability.

exercises.prompt: If an AI model outputs three completely different answers when asked the same question three times, what does this tell us?

options.text: The AI is extremely accurate and creative.
The AI's confidence is low, and it is likely guessing.
The AI is updating its internal knowledge base.
The AI is running out of memory space.

exercises.explain: If the AI keeps changing its mind every time you ask, it is generating random tokens and has low certainty about the fact.

exercises.prompt: To measure self-consistency, we must generate multiple answers from the AI instead of just relying on a single response.
exercises.explain: Self-consistency works by generating multiple distinct reasoning paths (runs) to see if they converge on the same conclusion.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word about AI consistency:
exercises.sentence: If an AI outputs the exact same answer across five different runs, we can feel more cons_blank about its truthfulness.
exercises.explain: When different reasoning paths yield the same result, the AI is consistent, making us more confident that the answer is accurate.

exercises.prompt: Which of these AI outputs shows the highest self-consistency?

options.text: Ask: 'Name a planet.' Run 1: Mars. Run 2: Venus. Run 3: Jupiter.
Ask: 'Who wrote Hamlet?' Run 1: Shakespeare. Run 2: Shakespeare. Run 3: Shakespeare.

exercises.explain: Generating the exact same answer across multiple runs shows high self-consistency, suggesting a reliable and factual output.

exercises.prompt: How does a self-consistency check help us measure if an AI is telling the truth or just guessing?

options.text: It forces the AI to output only one long answer to prevent errors.
It updates the AI's weight parameters to make it smarter permanently.
It checks if different reasoning runs lead to the same final answer to gauge certainty.
It compares the AI's answer directly against an online encyclopedia database.

exercises.explain: Self-consistency checks if multiple reasoning paths lead to the same answer. If the paths lead to widely different answers, the AI is likely hallucinating and cannot be trusted.
